quote: Hey. It's Bucky with Ion reaching out about the solar form you filled online. How you doing?
stewart_read: Bucky hits all three codex success signals for the standard intro variant: company name stated ('Ion'), form reference made ('solar form you filled online'), and a human pause invited ('How you doing?'). Clean, efficient, legitimacy-establishing. This is the call's strongest procedural moment.
coaching_implication: Credit this in the 1-on-1. The intro is textbook — Bucky knows how to open. The coaching conversation should contrast this clean start with the procedural gaps that follow, so the rep understands the issue is not confidence or legitimacy but execution depth in the back half.

quote: I know that one at a time is more. But I'd say, like, $2.50 average. Around $2.50 average, you said.
stewart_read: Aaron volunteers his average bill (~$250/month) and even flags that it spikes higher at times. Bucky confirms the number and moves on. The bill is captured but never flipped — no emotional alignment against the utility, no 'that's exactly why people are making this switch' bridge. Per the codex, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may be deferring the emotional flip to the closer, keeping his scope narrow. However, there is no evidence of deliberate deferral — he simply moves to the next verify item. Reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the codex's 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern versus his 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' A suggested intent (not Ion-canonical; pending Kenny's exemplar phrasings): something that turns Aaron's own number back against the utility — 'That's real money every month. That's exactly what the specialist is going to show you how to stop paying.' The flip doesn't require a fabricated savings figure; it uses Aaron's own stated pain.

quote: Yeah. It's on an app. She's paying it all electronically.
stewart_read: Aaron reveals that his wife manages the household finances — she controls the utility account. Combined with the earlier title confirmation (00:46), this is a clear signal that she is the financial co-decision-maker. Bucky treats it as a logistical detail about bill access and moves on. The correct move per the codex is to surface her role and confirm she will be on the appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may be planning to confirm her presence at button_up. However, he never does — the button_up contains no spouse-inclusion language. Reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-risk structural gap. Walk Bucky through the three-touch pattern: wife on title (00:46), wife as financial account manager (02:07), wife actively scheduling (05:24). Ask him: 'At which of these three moments did you know she needed to be on the closer call?' The answer should be the first one. Suggested intent (not Ion-canonical; pending Kenny's exemplar phrasings): at 02:07, something like 'Sounds like she's the one who knows the bill best — we should make sure she's on the call with us tomorrow so the specialist can walk you both through it.'

quote: Why do you need it all about it?
stewart_read: Aaron's question about why the bill is needed is a mild trust friction moment — he's not hostile, but he wants a reason. Bucky's response (01:45) explains the technical purpose (kWh graph) accurately and the friction resolves quickly. This is adequate handling, not a failure — but the explanation is functional rather than value-bridging. Bucky answers 'what the bill is for' rather than 'why this helps Aaron specifically.'
coaching_implication: The handling worked — Aaron cooperated immediately. The coaching point is the missed upgrade: Bucky could have used this moment to bridge from 'we need your bill' to 'this is how we make sure the design is built around your actual usage, not a guess.' Minor refinement, not a priority gap.

quote: I have metal. One part of newer. One part, a 100 years old.
stewart_read: Aaron discloses a split-age roof — one section new, one section approximately 100 years old. The 100-year-old section is a potential hard DQ flag (roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned) and a potential cross-sell signal (roof_replacement_planned). Bucky logs the answer and moves immediately to credit — no follow-up on whether the old section would need replacement, no bridge to Ion's in-house roofing capability.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may be leaving roof detail for the closer. However, the codex is explicit that cross-sell signals should be routed at setter stage, and a 100-year-old roof section is a material qualification flag that could affect whether the appointment is worth setting at all. Reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Two issues in one moment: (1) qualification risk — a 100-year-old roof section may require replacement before solar can be installed; Bucky should have asked 'Is there any plan to replace that older section?' to surface the DQ or the cross-sell. (2) cross-sell miss — if replacement is planned or needed, Ion does roofing in-house. This is the SESSION10 pattern. Ask Bucky: 'What would you have done if he said that section needs to come down before solar goes up?' Use it to introduce the roofing bridge.

stewart_read: A second voice — almost certainly the wife — actively participates in scheduling, offering time slots. She is present, engaged, and clearly involved in household decisions. Bucky books the 3PM slot without explicitly confirming she will be on the closer call. This is the third and final missed opportunity to lock in her presence on the appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: One could argue the wife's active participation in scheduling implies she will be on the call. However, 'helping pick a time' is not the same as 'confirmed on the appointment' — the closer needs an explicit commitment, not an inference. The codex is clear: co-decision-makers must be confirmed present.
coaching_implication: This is the most actionable of the three spouse-handling misses because the wife is literally on the line. Suggested intent (not Ion-canonical; pending Kenny's exemplar phrasings): 'It sounds like you're both there — perfect. We'll have you both on the call tomorrow at 3 so the specialist can walk you through everything together.' One sentence. Zero extra effort. Closes the structural gap.

quote: Alright. I'll call I'll text you after this to remind you, and then we'll call you tomorrow at 3PM. Okay?
stewart_read: Bucky confirms the appointment time and promises a reminder text — two of six required button_up elements. Missing: one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The text promise is present but will be generic without a tieback (per text_after_call requirements). The booking is real but the no-show protection scaffolding is almost entirely absent.
coaching_implication: Run Bucky through the six button_up elements against this call's close. He hit two. The 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/no-driving anchor are the highest-leverage missing pieces — both are directly predictive of show rate per the codex. This is a mechanical drill, not a philosophy conversation: have him practice the full button_up sequence on the next three calls and self-report which elements he hit.
